topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The oak door felt unnaturally heavy in my hand as I pushed it open. Sunlight, usually a warm, welcoming presence in the study, seemed to strike the empty room with a cold, clinical glare. Professor Alistair’s desk was immaculate, every pen perfectly aligned, every stack of research papers neatly stacked to the millimeter. There was no lingering scent of pipe tobacco, no half-finished cup of tea cooling on the coaster. Just silence, vast and absolute. I stood in the doorway, my breath catching somewhere in my chest, searching the space for any sign—a note, a forgotten book, anything that explained this sudden, stark void where my mentor used to preside. My fingers twitched, wanting to touch the smooth, polished wood of the empty chair opposite, but I hesitated, rooted to the spot, waiting for a sound that never came.

[story 2]

I found the official announcement tucked inside a seldom-used drawer—a crisp, impersonal card stating Alistair had officially stepped down from his post. It was the formality that snagged at my throat, the sheer lack of personal warmth in the typeface. I reread the words three times, my eyes tracing the elegant script, searching for the warmth I expected, the familiar cadence of his usual farewell. Nothing. I walked back to his office, the carpet muffling my footsteps. The air felt thin. I circled the desk slowly, my gaze snagging on a framed photograph of him smiling broadly at a conference years ago. I leaned in close, my reflection warping slightly in the glass, and felt a strange, hollow ache bloom behind my ribs. I didn't know how to process this sudden cessation of presence; my hands clenched into tight fists at my sides, betraying a frantic energy I couldn't quite channel.

[story 3]

"Professor?" I called out, my voice sounding too loud in the cavernous hallway. The apartment was silent, the usual low hum of his presence absent. I found his coat hanging by the door, neatly pressed, but there was no sign of him. I started systematically through his belongings, my movements jerky, as if navigating a minefield. His favorite armchair remained untouched, draped in a protective sheet. I picked up a leather-bound journal, flipping through the recent entries, but they ended abruptly, mid-sentence, as if the ink had simply stopped flowing mid-thought. A knot tightened in my stomach, a heavy, unfamiliar weight. I let the journal fall back onto the shelf, the sound echoing sharply. I stood still, my eyes wide, watching the dust motes dance in the single shaft of afternoon light, unable to move forward or retreat.
